.codex\I-anti-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:6065:.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:5088:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v2\PG_LB_FS_01_health.json:390:      "content": "A Comprehensive Perspective on the Biological Effects of Intermittent Fasting and Periodic Short-Term Fasting: A Promising Strategy for Optimizing Metabolic Health\nHighlights\n- Fasting exerts multidimensional effects, including triggering metabolic switching and improving immune function, redox balance, the adipokine profile, and mTOR pathway regulation, among others.\n- The modulation of the mTOR pathway through fasting shows promise, but there is a lack of consensus regarding its implementation; context-dependent regulation is crucial to avoid adverse effects and ensure safe clinical implementation.\n- Despite its documented metabolic benefits, prolonged short-term fasting (PSTF) remains under-researched and may be associated with mild, self-limiting symptoms that require appropriate monitoring.\n- A novel algorithm and protocol were developed to prepare healthy individuals for PSTF that lasted more than 24 hours and up to 72 hours.\n- Combining fasting with individualized physical activity is essential to preserve skeletal muscle mass and enhance overall metabolic outcomes.\nAbstract\n1. Introduction\n2. Impact of Various Fasting Regimens on Metabolic and Cellular Signaling Pathways\n2.1. Overview of Different Intermittent Fasting Patterns\n2.2. Effect of Various TRE Schemes on Metabolic and Signaling Pathways\n2.3. Effect of Various PSTF Schemes on Metabolic and Signaling Pathways\n| Fasting Time Frame | Duration | Study Design | Study Population | Positive Metabolic Outcomes | Negative Metabolic Outcomes | First Author/[Ref.] |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 24 h | 10 weeks | Single-center, randomized controlled trial | 88 females overweight females | ↓ glucose, ↓ insulin, ↓ HOMA-IR, ↑ BHB | ↓ insulin sensitivity, ↑ AST | Hutchison et al., 2019 [58] |\n| 24 h | Single protocol | Randomized controlled crossover trial | 30 healthy cases (20 women and 10 men) | ↑ GH, the observed changes in GH levels occurred independently of any associated weight loss | Not reported | Horne et al., 2025 [60] |\n| 24 h | 26 weeks | Randomized controlled trial | 38 adults | ↓ HOMA-IR, ↓ insulin, ↓ fasting glucose, ↓ metabolic syndrome score, ↓ diastolic blood pressure, ↑ HDL-C | Not reported | Bartholomew et al., 2021 [61] |\n| 24 h | 26 weeks | Randomized controlled trial | 68 adults | ↓ HOMA-IR, ↓ insulin, ↓ fasting glucose | ↔ body weight | Horne et al., 2024 [62] |\n| 24 h | 4 weeks | Randomized controlled trial | 28 metabolically healthy, non-obese adults | ↓ body weight, ↑ BHB, ↓ sICAM-1, ↓ systolic blood pressure, ↓ diastolic blood pressure, ↓ HR, ↓ fat: lean ratios, improved lipide profile | ↓ T3 | Stekovic et al., 2019 [63] |\n| 24 h | 8 weeks | Randomized parallel-arm trial | 8 healthy adults (4 male and 4 female) | ↓ body weight, ↓ BMI, ↓ FFM | ↓ SMM | Herz et al., 2024 [51] |\n| 36 h | Single protocol | Randomized crossover study | 20 healthy adults (11 male and 9 female) | ↑ BHB, ↓insulin, ↑ glucagon, ↓ insulin/glucagon ratio | Not reported | Deru et al., 2021 [64] |\n| 36 h | Single protocol | Controlled pilot study | 20 healthy adults (10 male and 10 female) | ↓ TGs, ↓glucose, ↑ circulating ketone bodies, ↑ total antioxidant capacity | Not reported | Rhodes et al., 2023 [15] |\n| 48 h | Single protocol | Randomized crossover study | 11 overweight/obese women (mean age: 68.8 ± 6.4 years) | ↓ body mass, ↓ BMI, ↓ FFM, ↓ glucose, ↑ ketone bodies | ↑ salivary cortisol concentration, ↑ hunger, ↑ fatigue, ↑ tension, ↓ reaction time | Solianik et al., 2020 [65] |\n| 48 h | Single protocol | Paired within-subject design | 16 healthy females | ↓ body mass, ↓ BMI, ↔ in cortisol levels | Not reported | Mazurak et al., 2013 [66] |\n| 48 h | Single protocol | Paired within-subject design | 9 healthy male participants | ↓ body mass, ↓ BMI, ↓glucose, ↓ systolic blood pressure, ↓ heart rate, ↓ oxygenated hemoglobin | ↑ anger, ↓ reaction time | Solianik et al., 2016 [67] |\n| 59 h | Single protocol | Controlled human study | 15 young, healthy, non-obese subjects (11 men and 4 women) | ↓ glucose, ↓ insulin, ↑ GH, ↑ lipolytic rate | Not reported | Goldenberg et al., 2022 [68] |\n| 60 h | Separated by a period of minimally 2 weeks. | Randomized controlled crossover study | 12 healthy male participants | ↓ body weight, ↓ RER, ↓ energy expenditure | Not reported | Andriessen et al., 2023 [69] |\n| 60 h | 2 experimental periods | Randomized controlled crossover study | 12 healthy male participants | ↑ FFA, ↓ insulin, ↓ glucose, ↑ β-oxidation of FFA | ↓ skeletal muscle insulin sensitivity | Hoeks et al., 2010 [70] |\n| 60 h | 2 experimental periods | Randomized crossover study | 10 healthy male participants | ↓ leptin, ↓ chemerin, ↑ VEGF | ↔ in Il-1, Il-6, TNF-α,IL-8, hs-CR, adiponectin levels | van Herpen et al., 2013 [71] |\n| 72 h | Single protocol | FAST*BRAIN study | 15 healthy females | ↓ body weight, ↓ glucose, ↑ ketone bodies | ↑ BDI-2 score | Ding et al., 2018 [59] |\n| 72 h | Two analyses were conducted | Randomized crossover study | 8 healthy males | ↓ insulin, ↓ C-peptide, ↓ glucose, ↓ mTOR, ↑ FFA, ↑ glucagon, ↑ blood flow | ↓ free and total T3 | Vendelbo et al., 2014 [72] |\n| 72 h | Single protocol | Controlled metabolic study (lean vs. obese, pre–post design) | 18 healthy men, subjects (9 normal-weight and 9 obese) | ↓ insulin, ↓ C-peptide, ↓ glucose, ↑ FFA, ↑ glucagon,↔ in cortisol levels, ↓ mTOR, ↑ p62 | Not reported | Bak et al., 2016 [73] |\n3. Algorithm and Protocol for Preparing Healthy Individuals for PSTF Lasting More than 24 h and up to 72 h\n4. A Graphical Summary of the Effects of PSTF Lasting More than 24 h and up to 72 h\n5. General Discussion and Future Directions\n6. Methodology\n- Cross-sectional, case–control, and randomized control trials, and retrospective, prospective cohort or cohort epidemiological studies were included.\n- Only studies conducted in humans were included.\n- Only full-text articles in English were included.\n- Non-human experimental models were excluded.\n- Non-English papers were excluded because of the structural language barrier.\n- Articles that were not peer-reviewed were excluded.\n- Studies involving fasting periods for religious reasons or those exceeding 72 h were also omitted.\n- Studies classified as meta-analyses or systematic reviews were excluded.\n7. .codex\I-anti-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:6066:.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:5093:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v1\PG_LB_FS_01_health.json:385:      "content": "A Comprehensive Perspective on the Biological Effects of Intermittent Fasting and Periodic Short-Term Fasting: A Promising Strategy for Optimizing Metabolic Health\nHighlights\n- Fasting exerts multidimensional effects, including triggering metabolic switching and improving immune function, redox balance, the adipokine profile, and mTOR pathway regulation, among others.\n- The modulation of the mTOR pathway through fasting shows promise, but there is a lack of consensus regarding its implementation; context-dependent regulation is crucial to avoid adverse effects and ensure safe clinical implementation.\n- Despite its documented metabolic benefits, prolonged short-term fasting (PSTF) remains under-researched and may be associated with mild, self-limiting symptoms that require appropriate monitoring.\n- A novel algorithm and protocol were developed to prepare healthy individuals for PSTF that lasted more than 24 hours and up to 72 hours.\n- Combining fasting with individualized physical activity is essential to preserve skeletal muscle mass and enhance overall metabolic outcomes.\nAbstract\n1. Introduction\n2. Impact of Various Fasting Regimens on Metabolic and Cellular Signaling Pathways\n2.1. Overview of Different Intermittent Fasting Patterns\n2.2. Effect of Various TRE Schemes on Metabolic and Signaling Pathways\n2.3. Effect of Various PSTF Schemes on Metabolic and Signaling Pathways\n| Fasting Time Frame | Duration | Study Design | Study Population | Positive Metabolic Outcomes | Negative Metabolic Outcomes | First Author/[Ref.] |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 24 h | 10 weeks | Single-center, randomized controlled trial | 88 females overweight females | ↓ glucose, ↓ insulin, ↓ HOMA-IR, ↑ BHB | ↓ insulin sensitivity, ↑ AST | Hutchison et al., 2019 [58] |\n| 24 h | Single protocol | Randomized controlled crossover trial | 30 healthy cases (20 women and 10 men) | ↑ GH, the observed changes in GH levels occurred independently of any associated weight loss | Not reported | Horne et al., 2025 [60] |\n| 24 h | 26 weeks | Randomized controlled trial | 38 adults | ↓ HOMA-IR, ↓ insulin, ↓ fasting glucose, ↓ metabolic syndrome score, ↓ diastolic blood pressure, ↑ HDL-C | Not reported | Bartholomew et al., 2021 [61] |\n| 24 h | 26 weeks | Randomized controlled trial | 68 adults | ↓ HOMA-IR, ↓ healthy adults (4 male and 4 female) | ↓ body weight, ↓ BMI, ↓ FFM | ↓ SMM | Herz et al., 2024 [51] |\n| 36 h | Single protocol | Randomized crossover study | 20 healthy adults (11 male and 9 female) FFA | ↓ skeletal muscle insulin sensitivity | Hoeks et al., 2010 [70] |\n| 60 h | 2 experimental periods | Randomized crossover study | 10 healthy male participants | ↓ leptin, ↓ chemerin, ↑ VEGF | ↔ in Il-1, Il-6, TNF-α,IL-8, hs-CR, adiponectin levels | van Herpen et al., 2013 [71] |\n| 72 h | Single protocol | FAST*BRAIN study | 15 healthy females | ↓ body weight, ↓ glucose, ↑ ketone bodies | ↑ BDI-2 score | Ding et al., 2018 [59] |\n| 72 h | Two analyses were conducted | Randomized crossover study | 8 healthy males | ↓ insulin, ↓ C-peptide, ↓ glucose, ↓ mTOR, ↑ FFA, ↑ glucagon, ↑ blood flow | ↓ free and total T3 | Vendelbo et al., 2014 [72] |\n| 72 h | Single protocol | Controlled metabolic study (lean vs. obese, pre–post design) | 18 healthy men, subjects (9 normal-weight and 9 obese) | ↓ insulin, ↓ C-peptide, ↓ glucose, ↑ FFA, ↑ glucagon,↔ in cortisol levels, ↓ mTOR, ↑ p62 | Not reported | Bak et al., 2016 [73] |\n3. Algorithm and Protocol for Preparing Healthy Individuals for PSTF Lasting More than 24 h and up to 72 h\n4. A Graphical Summary of the Effects of PSTF Lasting More than 24 h and up to 72 h\n5. General Discussion and Future Directions\n6. Methodology\n- Cross-sectional, case–control, and randomized control trials, and retrospective, prospective cohort or cohort epidemiological studies were included.\n- Only studies conducted in humans were included.\n- Only full-text articles in English were included.\n- Non-human experimental models were excluded.\n- Non-English papers were excluded because of the structural language barrier.\n- Articles that were not peer-reviewed were excluded.\n- Studies involving fasting periods for religious reasons or those exceeding 72 h were also omitted.\n- Studies classified as meta-analyses or systematic reviews were excluded.\n7. .codex\I-anti-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:9386:.\outputs\codex_findings\md1_harness_review\codex_stdout.log:3300:vs = ValidationSet(in_scope=(ValidationCase(case_id='c1', group='in_scope', query='q', curator_contract_slug='stub'),), ambiguous=(), out_of_scope=())
.codex\I-anti-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:9388:.\outputs\codex_findings\md7_phase1_v2_review\codex_stdout.log:2409:outputs\polaris_graph\GEMINI_E2E_20260316_015917_report.md:1:# Comparative Analysis of Adhesion Testing Methods for Polymer Coatings: Mechanisms, Standards, and Performance ## Abstract This research report presents a comparative analysis of adhesion testing methods for polymer coatings, specifically evaluating pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. Spanning 15 sections and comprising 13,013 words, the study examines regulatory frameworks, methodological scopes, and performance thresholds inherent to current industry standards. The methodology involves a comprehensive review of 61 unique sources, accumulating 179 total citations to substantiate findings regarding test reliability and validity. Key findings indicate significant variability in cross-cut tape method reliability and highlight the critical influence of peel test shear rate, modulus, thickness, and configuration on outcome accuracy. Furthermore, the analysis identifies substrate condition, curing factors, and surface preparation wettability mechanisms as primary determinants of bond failure modes. The report assesses alternative scrape and bend testing methods while classifying adhesion failure modes to enhance measurement unit validity despite coating contamination risks. Implications suggest that while qualitative methods offer comparative accuracy, strict adherence to standardized lattice patterns and adhesive composition validation is essential for consistent performance assessment. This synthesis provides a robust framework for optimizing adhesion testing protocols, ensuring enhanced durability and compliance within polymer coating applications through rigorous methodological scrutiny. ## Regulatory Framework and Methodological Scope The regulatory framework governing adhesion testing for polymer coatings establishes distinct boundaries for methodological application, particularly when evaluating epoxy, silicone, and urethane material systems. Furthermore, adhesion tests measure how well a coating or paint sticks to a substrate, focusing on durability and resistance to peeling [1]. In addition, the scope of this analysis encompasses three primary mechanical evaluation methods: pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests, each governed by specific industry standards that dictate their validity across different substrate conditions [2]. Portable adhesion testers operate in accordance with ISO 4624 or ASTM-D-4541 industry standards for quantifying coating adhesion [2]. These standards define the procedural limits within which data remains comparable, ensuring that structural integrity and corrosion protection parameters are accurately assessed [3]. Consequently, test results are not comparable between methods because they assess different adhesion properties and utilize different mechanisms [4]. This limitation is central to the regulatory framework, as selecting the wrong method for a specific polymer coating invalidates compliance data [4]. In addition, pull-off adhesion testing applies tensile force perpendicular to the substrate to measure coating resistance to separation [1]. This method is widely recognized as the most accurate and reliable method for quantitatively measuring adhesion strength [5]. The test involves adhering a dolly to the coating and applying force until the coating fails [5]. Test results provide a quantitative adhesion value expressed in units like MPa, kg/m², N/mm², or PSI [6]. ASTM D4541 is the standard used for adhesion pull-off strength testing of coating systems [7]. Conflicting evidence exists regarding the specific substrate application of pull-off standards. One source states ASTM D7234 specifies pull-off adhesion strength testing for coatings on concrete [8]. Another source indicates ASTM D-4541 is the standard for pull-off adhesion testing of coatings on large diameter steel water pipe [9]. A third piece of evidence reiterates that ASTM D4541 is the standard used for adhesion pull-off strength testing of coating systems generally [7]. The disagreement suggests a specialization of standards where ASTM D7234 targets concrete substrates specifically, while ASTM D4541 applies to broader coating systems including steel pipes [8]. Additionally, this distinction is critical for the research question, as epoxy and urethane coatings on concrete require different regulatory compliance than those on steel [8]. The pull-off test is destructive and requires repairing the removed coating section [10]. Both test techniques were limited by the strength of the epoxy adhesive bonding pull-off stubs [11]. Additionally, correctly mixed glue shows an average failure value of 6.67 MPa in pull-off tests [12]. Attaching test dolly to partially cured coating may retard cure and negatively affect test results [13]. Cross-cut testing offers a different methodological approach, assessing coating resistance to separation from the surface after cuts are applied down to the substrate level [14]. ISO 2409 provides a standardized method for assessing resistance of paint coatings to separation from substrates using the cross-cut test [15]. ASTM D3359 outlines standardized test methods for evaluating adhesion of coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape [16]. Adhesion is assessed based on a 0A–5A scale, where 5A indicates no peeling and 0A complete removal [16]. The cross-cut test is not suitable for coatings of total thickness greater than 250 micrometers [17]. Evidence regarding the descriptive nature of ISO 2409 presents a conflict. One source states ISO 2409 specifies a test method for assessing resistance of paint coatings to separation from substrates using right-angle cuts [18]. Another source claims ISO 2409 provides a standardized method for assessing resistance of paint coatings to separation from substrates using the cross-cut test [15]. A third source notes ISO 2409 cross-cut test procedure is not a means of measuring adhesion according to NOTE 1 [17]. The preponderance of evidence supports the classification of ISO 2409 as a cross-cut method, but the note regarding measurement implies it is qualitative rather than quantitative [17]. This aligns with findings that the cross-cut test only provides a general indication of adhesion quality lacking the precision of quantitative methods [5]. Test Method B (Cross-Cut) uses 11 cuts spaced 1 mm apart for coatings up to 2.0 mils thick [10]. Cross-cut test rates adhesion based on percentage of affected area: 5%, 15%, 35%, and 65% thresholds [19]. ASTM D3359 allows semi-quantification of coating adhesion to the specific substrate it is applied to [20]. The tape test is qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesive [19]. Peel testing provides data on bond strength between adhesive and substrate [8]. This method measures the force required to strip away a flexible coating or material from a substrate [1]. Peel tests measure adhesive strengths of at least one flexible material bonded to another flexible or rigid material [21]. ASTM D903 defines the test method for determining peel or stripping strength of adhesive bonds [22]. Configuration variables significantly influence results, with the 90-degree peel test used for rigid adherends and peels at a right angle [23]. ISO 8510-2 covers adhesives peel test for flexible-bonded-to-rigid test specimen assembly 180 degree peel [21]. Conflict exists regarding the application of peel angles. One source states the 180-degree peel test is used for flexible adherends and peels material back onto itself. The opposing evidence states the 90-degree peel test is used for rigid adherends and peels at a right angle [23]. These positions are not mutually exclusive but rather complementary, defining the scope of peel testing based on substrate rigidity [23]. For silicone materials, peel test kits measure the force required to pull the coating off the primed surface [24]. Silicones rarely exceed 2 MPa in structural strength testing compared to epoxies [25]. This performance threshold informs the selection of testing methods, as quantitative pull-off tests yield low values for silicone compared to epoxy systems [25]. Moreover, the T shaped peel test ASTM D1876 is the most widely used peel test for thin-gauge metal adherends [26]. Standardized adhesive peel tests are conducted on clean, contaminant free substrates for quantitative results [26]. Peel adhesion strength is calculated as the force required to peel an adhesive with a specific width from the substrate [27]. Japanese Industrial Standards (JIS) provide additional regulatory scope. JIS K 5600-5-6: 1999 specifies the Cross-Cut Test method for coating adhesion evaluation [28]. JIS K 5600-5-7: 2014 specifies the Pull-Off Method for coating tensile adhesion testing [28]. This distinction clarifies the conflict between the two JIS standards, assigning them to different mechanical actions (cross-cutting vs. Pulling) [28]. Regulatory thresholds for thickness also constrain method selection. These methods are limited to coatings with a thickness of ≤ 125 µm (5 mils) unless agreed otherwise [16]. Typical dry film thicknesses of conformal coatings range from 1-5 mils (25.4-127μm) [29]. Adhesion testing is required in areas indicating worn substrates, substrate changes, ponding water, or previous coatings [30]. The service provider can perform adhesion testing to quantify coating to substrate or intercoat adhesion characteristics [31]. Hot water and knife adhesion tests proved valuable for quality control and in-depth studies of coating adhesion [32]. Good agreement was observed between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel test results [32]. Polyaspartic coating published data sheet shows 900 psi adhesion strength [7]. Adhesion tests conducted on INSTRON universal testing machine with 30 kN maximum load capacity [33]. The facility features equipment units for adhesion testing appropriate to the methodology required by the standard [31]. The scope of this report excludes methods that do not align with the quantitative or standardized qualitative requirements defined above [4]. Scratch testing uses sphero-conical stylus with tip radius range of 1 to 200 micrometers [34]. Contact angle measurement is a quantitative, objective measurement that predicts adhesion [35]. Ionic contamination tests do not detect non-ionic contamination which is the source of adhesion issues [35]. Environmental testing evaluates bond durability under elevated and low temperatures, moisture, humidity [36]. Bend tests were not reliable indicators of coating adhesion and measured coating flexibility instead [32]. | Test Method | Primary Standard | Measurement Type | Max Thickness Limit | Substrate Applicability | |:--- |:--- |:--- |:--- |:--- | | Pull-Off | ASTM D4541 / ISO 4624 | Quantitative (MPa, PSI) [6] | Not specified in evidence | Concrete, Steel, General | | Cross-Cut | ASTM D3359 / ISO 2409 | Qualitative (0A-5A) [16] | 250 micrometers [17] | Metallic, Dry Coatings | | Peel (90/180) | ASTM D903 / ISO 8510-2 | Quantitative (Force/Width) [27] | ~200 micrometers [37] | Flexible, Rigid, Silicone [24] | | Peel (T-Shape) | ASTM D1876 | Quantitative (Force) [26] | Thin-gauge metal [26] | Metal Adherends | *Table 1: Comparison of Adhesion Testing Methods by Standard and Applicability* In summary, the regulatory framework delineates clear boundaries between quantitative pull-off and peel methods versus qualitative cross-cut techniques. ASTM D4541 and ISO 4624 govern tensile separation, while ASTM D3359 and ISO 2409 govern lattice cutting. Moreover, peel tests bridge flexible and rigid substrate testing through ASTM D903 and D1876. Conflicts in standard application are resolved by substrate specificity, such as concrete versus steel for pul…17125 tokens truncated…al System | Surface Preparation Requirement | Adhesion Characteristics | Testing Implications |. | Urethane Adhesives | Typically do not require surface preparation [52] | Bonds similar and dissimilar substrates [52] | Lower preparation variability |. | Epoxy Coatings | Rigorous preparation required | Strong structural-grade bonds | Can form surface carbamates |. | Silicone Systems | Process control critical | Low adhesion strength | Failures from process issues |. | Low Surface Energy | Primer treatment required | Naturally resist adhesion | Wetting limitations |. The influence of surface preparation extends to the physical configuration of the test specimen, particularly regarding coating thickness. Therefore, thickness control is a component of surface preparation that ensures the failure occurs at the intended interface. Variations in testing standards introduce further complexity to surface preparation requirements. These substrate-specific standards imply different preparation needs; concrete requires different surface profiling than steel pipe. The existence of multiple standards for similar measurements necessitates strict adherence to the specific preparation guidelines within each standard to maintain data validity. The method of testing also dictates the sensitivity to surface preparation. This method is sensitive to the bond line quality established during preparation. For instance, a surface prepared for peel testing might not yield valid cross-cut results if the cutting tool interacts differently with the prepared profile. Chemical bonding mechanisms further differentiate the performance of polymer coatings under test. Adhesion can result from Van Der Waals forces and diffuse bonding with chemical reactions at interface. Epoxy adhesives perform well in stable environments and can withstand temperatures up to around 150°C [60]. This disparity suggests that surface preparation alone cannot overcome intrinsic material limitations in silicone systems without primers or treatments. Without this control, the comparison between epoxy, urethane, and silicone becomes invalid as the variable of surface readiness remains uncontrolled. The validity of the test is also contingent on the removal of the coating during measurement. Coating removal is used to determine the rate of adhesion of the coating or paint. If the preparation of the dolly bond is inferior to the coating-substrate bond, the test measures the preparation of the test fixture rather than the coating. Therefore, surface preparation is not merely a preliminary step but a critical variable that defines the upper limit of measurable adhesion strength. Environmental factors interacting with surface preparation also influence long-term validity. Surface preparation mitigates some of these risks by ensuring a stable interface. These conditions represent deviations from ideal surface preparation, requiring adjusted testing protocols to account for the degraded interface. In summary, surface preparation and wettability are foundational to the comparative analysis of adhesion testing methods. The evidence indicates that while urethane systems offer flexibility in preparation, epoxy and silicone systems demand rigorous control to prevent process-induced failures. Contaminants, thickness uniformity, and standard-specific requirements all converge at the interface to determine the outcome of pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests. Ignoring these mechanisms renders quantitative comparisons between polymer coatings unreliable. * Contact angle measurement provides an objective, quantitative prediction of adhesion potential by ensuring sufficient spreading of the coating formulation. * Material-specific preparation requirements vary, with urethane adhesives typically requiring no preparation while low surface energy materials like Teflon require primer treatment. * Discrepancies in standards for different substrates, such as concrete versus steel pipe, imply varied preparation protocols must be followed to ensure test validity. ## References [1] Rohit Mishra (2025). .codex\I-anti-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:9389:.\outputs\codex_findings\md7_phase1_v2_review\codex_stdout.log:2456:outputs\polaris_graph\GEMINI_E2E_20260316_015917_report.md:1:# Comparative Analysis of Adhesion Testing Methods for Polymer Coatings: Mechanisms, Standards, and Performance ## Abstract This research report presents a comparative analysis of adhesion testing methods for polymer coatings, specifically evaluating pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. Spanning 15 sections and comprising 13,013 words, the study examines regulatory frameworks, methodological scopes, and performance thresholds inherent to current industry standards. The methodology involves a comprehensive review of 61 unique sources, accumulating 179 total citations to substantiate findings regarding test reliability and validity. Key findings indicate significant variability in cross-cut tape method reliability and highlight the critical influence of peel test shear rate, modulus, thickness, and configuration on outcome accuracy. Furthermore, the analysis identifies substrate condition, curing factors, and surface preparation wettability mechanisms as primary determinants of bond failure modes. The report assesses alternative scrape and bend testing methods while classifying adhesion failure modes to enhance measurement unit validity despite coating contamination risks. Implications suggest that while qualitative methods offer comparative accuracy, strict adherence to standardized lattice patterns and adhesive composition validation is essential for consistent performance assessment. This synthesis provides a robust framework for optimizing adhesion testing protocols, ensuring enhanced durability and compliance within polymer coating applications through rigorous methodological scrutiny. ## Regulatory Framework and Methodological Scope The regulatory framework governing adhesion testing for polymer coatings establishes distinct boundaries for methodological application, particularly when evaluating epoxy, silicone, and urethane material systems. Furthermore, adhesion tests measure how well a coating or paint sticks to a substrate, focusing on durability and resistance to peeling [1]. In addition, the scope of this analysis encompasses three primary mechanical evaluation methods: pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests, each governed by specific industry standards that dictate their validity across different substrate conditions [2]. Portable adhesion testers operate in accordance with ISO 4624 or ASTM-D-4541 industry standards for quantifying coating adhesion [2]. These standards define the procedural limits within which data remains comparable, ensuring that structural integrity and corrosion protection parameters are accurately assessed [3]. Consequently, test results are not comparable between methods because they assess different adhesion properties and utilize different mechanisms [4]. This limitation is central to the regulatory framework, as selecting the wrong method for a specific polymer coating invalidates compliance data [4]. In addition, pull-off adhesion testing applies tensile force perpendicular to the substrate to measure coating resistance to separation [1]. This method is widely recognized as the most accurate and reliable method for quantitatively measuring adhesion strength [5]. The test involves adhering a dolly to the coating and applying force until the coating fails [5]. Test results provide a quantitative adhesion value expressed in units like MPa, kg/m², N/mm², or PSI [6]. ASTM D4541 is the standard used for adhesion pull-off strength testing of coating systems [7]. Conflicting evidence exists regarding the specific substrate application of pull-off standards. One source states ASTM D7234 specifies pull-off adhesion strength testing for coatings on concrete [8]. Another source indicates ASTM D-4541 is the standard for pull-off adhesion testing of coatings on large diameter steel water pipe [9]. A third piece of evidence reiterates that ASTM D4541 is the standard used for adhesion pull-off strength testing of coating systems generally [7]. The disagreement suggests a specialization of standards where ASTM D7234 targets concrete substrates specifically, while ASTM D4541 applies to broader coating systems including steel pipes [8]. Additionally, this distinction is critical for the research question, as epoxy and urethane coatings on concrete require different regulatory compliance than those on steel [8]. The pull-off test is destructive and requires repairing the removed coating section [10]. Both test techniques were limited by the strength of the epoxy adhesive bonding pull-off stubs [11]. Additionally, correctly mixed glue shows an average failure value of 6.67 MPa in pull-off tests [12]. Attaching test dolly to partially cured coating may retard cure and negatively affect test results [13]. Cross-cut testing offers a different methodological approach, assessing coating resistance to separation from the surface after cuts are applied down to the substrate level [14]. ISO 2409 provides a standardized method for assessing resistance of paint coatings to separation from substrates using the cross-cut test [15]. ASTM D3359 outlines standardized test methods for evaluating adhesion of coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape [16]. Adhesion is assessed based on a 0A–5A scale, where 5A indicates no peeling and 0A complete removal [16]. The cross-cut test is not suitable for coatings of total thickness greater than 250 micrometers [17]. Evidence regarding the descriptive nature of ISO 2409 presents a conflict. One source states ISO 2409 specifies a test method for assessing resistance of paint coatings to separation from substrates using right-angle cuts [18]. Another source claims ISO 2409 provides a standardized method for assessing resistance of paint coatings to separation from substrates using the cross-cut test [15]. A third source notes ISO 2409 cross-cut test procedure is not a means of measuring adhesion according to NOTE 1 [17]. The preponderance of evidence supports the classification of ISO 2409 as a cross-cut method, but the note regarding measurement implies it is qualitative rather than quantitative [17]. This aligns with findings that the cross-cut test only provides a general indication of adhesion quality lacking the precision of quantitative methods [5]. Test Method B (Cross-Cut) uses 11 cuts spaced 1 mm apart for coatings up to 2.0 mils thick [10]. Cross-cut test rates adhesion based on percentage of affected area: 5%, 15%, 35%, and 65% thresholds [19]. ASTM D3359 allows semi-quantification of coating adhesion to the specific substrate it is applied to [20]. The tape test is qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesive [19]. Peel testing provides data on bond strength between adhesive and substrate [8]. This method measures the force required to strip away a flexible coating or material from a substrate [1]. Peel tests measure adhesive strengths of at least one flexible material bonded to another flexible or rigid material [21]. ASTM D903 defines the test method for determining peel or stripping strength of adhesive bonds [22]. Configuration variables significantly influence results, with the 90-degree peel test used for rigid adherends and peels at a right angle [23]. ISO 8510-2 covers adhesives peel test for flexible-bonded-to-rigid test specimen assembly 180 degree peel [21]. Conflict exists regarding the application of peel angles. One source states the 180-degree peel test is used for flexible adherends and peels material back onto itself. The opposing evidence states the 90-degree peel test is used for rigid adherends and peels at a right angle [23]. These positions are not mutually exclusive but rather complementary, defining the scope of peel testing based on substrate rigidity [23]. For silicone materials, peel test kits measure the force required to pull the coating off the primed surface [24]. Silicones rarely exceed 2 MPa in structural strength testing compared to epoxies [25]. This performance threshold informs the selection of testing methods, as quantitative pull-off tests yield low values for silicone compared to epoxy systems [25]. Moreover, the T shaped peel test ASTM D1876 is the most widely used peel test for thin-gauge metal adherends [26]. Standardized adhesive peel tests are conducted on clean, contaminant free substrates for quantitative results [26]. Peel adhesion strength is calculated as the force required to peel an adhesive with a specific width from the substrate [27]. Japanese Industrial Standards (JIS) provide additional regulatory scope. JIS K 5600-5-6: 1999 specifies the Cross-Cut Test method for coating adhesion evaluation [28]. JIS K 5600-5-7: 2014 specifies the Pull-Off Method for coating tensile adhesion testing [28]. This distinction clarifies the conflict between the two JIS standards, assigning them to different mechanical actions (cross-cutting vs. Pulling) [28]. Regulatory thresholds for thickness also constrain method selection. These methods are limited to coatings with a thickness of ≤ 125 µm (5 mils) unless agreed otherwise [16]. Typical dry film thicknesses of conformal coatings range from 1-5 mils (25.4-127μm) [29]. Adhesion testing is required in areas indicating worn substrates, substrate changes, ponding water, or previous coatings [30]. The service provider can perform adhesion testing to quantify coating to substrate or intercoat adhesion characteristics [31]. Hot water and knife adhesion tests proved valuable for quality control and in-depth studies of coating adhesion [32]. Good agreement was observed between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel test results [32]. Polyaspartic coating published data sheet shows 900 psi adhesion strength [7]. Adhesion tests conducted on INSTRON universal testing machine with 30 kN maximum load capacity [33]. The facility features equipment units for adhesion testing appropriate to the methodology required by the standard [31]. The scope of this report excludes methods that do not align with the quantitative or standardized qualitative requirements defined above [4]. Scratch testing uses sphero-conical stylus with tip radius range of 1 to 200 micrometers [34]. Contact angle measurement is a quantitative, objective measurement that predicts adhesion [35]. Ionic contamination tests do not detect non-ionic contamination which is the source of adhesion issues [35]. Environmental testing evaluates bond durability under elevated and low temperatures, moisture, humidity [36]. Bend tests were not reliable indicators of coating adhesion and measured coating flexibility instead [32]. | Test Method | Primary Standard | Measurement Type | Max Thickness Limit | Substrate Applicability | |:--- |:--- |:--- |:--- |:--- | | Pull-Off | ASTM D4541 / ISO 4624 | Quantitative (MPa, PSI) [6] | Not specified in evidence | Concrete, Steel, General | | Cross-Cut | ASTM D3359 / ISO 2409 | Qualitative (0A-5A) [16] | 250 micrometers [17] | Metallic, Dry Coatings | | Peel (90/180) | ASTM D903 / ISO 8510-2 | Quantitative (Force/Width) [27] | ~200 micrometers [37] | Flexible, Rigid, Silicone [24] | | Peel (T-Shape) | ASTM D1876 | Quantitative (Force) [26] | Thin-gauge metal [26] | Metal Adherends | *Table 1: Comparison of Adhesion Testing Methods by Standard and Applicability* In summary, the regulatory framework delineates clear boundaries between quantitative pull-off and peel methods versus qualitative cross-cut techniques. ASTM D4541 and ISO 4624 govern tensile separation, while ASTM D3359 and ISO 2409 govern lattice cutting. Moreover, peel tests bridge flexible and rigid substrate testing through ASTM D903 and D1876. Conflicts in standard application are resolved by substrate specificity, such as concrete versus steel for pull-off tests. For instance, material performance thresholds, such as the the reported value limit for silicones, further inform method selection. Thickness limitations restrict cross-cut methods to coatings under 250 micrometers. These constraints define the methodological scope for comparing adhesion testing across polymer coatings. Nevertheless, **Key Findings** * Pull-off testing (ASTM D4541/ISO 4624) provides quantitative data in MPa or PSI but is limited by the strength of the adhesive bonding the test dolly [11]. * Cross-cut testing (ASTM D3359/ISO 2409) is restricted to coatings thinner than 250 micrometers and offers qualitative 0A-5A ratings rather than precise strength values. * Peel test configurations vary by substrate rigidity, with 90-degree tests for rigid adherends and 180-degree tests for flexible materials [23]. * Silicone coatings typically exhibit lower structural strength (<2 MPa) compared to epoxies, influencing the choice of quantitative versus qualitative testing methods [25]. * Test results between methods are not directly comparable due to differing mechanical mechanisms and adhesion properties assessed [4]. ## Cross-Cut Lattice Pattern Specifications Regulatory frameworks establish the geometric configurations and tooling requirements for cross-cut testing, dictating practical application across polymer coating systems. In contrast, both standards utilize cutting mechanisms to induce failure, yet specific lattice patterns and tooling mandates differ significantly, influencing the reliability of results for materials such as epoxy, urethane, and silicone. The divergence in standard specifications necessitates a detailed comparison of mandated cut geometries and evaluation metrics. ASTM D3359 differentiates between field and laboratory applications through distinct method classifications, whereas ISO 2409 maintains a consistent lattice approach. The following table summarizes critical specification parameters derived from the governing standards. | Specification Parameter | ASTM D3359 Method A | ASTM D3359 Method B | ISO 2409 Standard |. | **Cut Geometry** | X-shaped cut | 11 cuts spaced 1 mm apart [10] | Right-angle lattice pattern |. | **Cut Density** | Single intersection [38] | 1 mm spacing [10] | Six parallel and six perpendicular cuts [15] |. | **Thickness Constraint** | Field use designation | Up to 2.0 mils thick | ≤ 125 µm or 250 µm limit |. | **Evaluation Metric** | 0B to 5B classification [19] | 0B to 5B classification [19] | Percentage area thresholds |. Selection between an X-shaped cut and a lattice pattern introduces trade-offs in stress concentration and failure mode visibility. Test Method A is designed primarily for field use and involves making an X-shaped cut in the coating. This configuration allows for rapid assessment on gently curved surfaces without cutting test panels, making it suitable for in-situ evaluation of large structures [28]. The lattice pattern mandated by ISO 2409 and ASTM D3359 Method B provides a more granular assessment of adhesion quality. The cross-cut test involves making six parallel cuts and another six perpendicular cuts penetrating through to the substrate. This density creates a grid of 25 squares, allowing for a percentage-based calculation of coating removal. Cross-hatch adhesion testing uses a multiblade tool to scribe into the coating down to the substrate, ensuring uniform spacing that single-blade X-cuts cannot guarantee [39]. For polymer coatings like urethanes or epoxies where uniformity impacts adhesion performance characteristics along with durability and chemical resistance, the precision of the multiblade tool is critical [40]. A significant conflict exists regarding the maximum coating thickness suitable for cross-cut testing, which directly impacts the testing of thicker polymer systems. This discrepancy creates ambiguity for testing thick-film epoxies or high-build urethanes. Nevertheless, the ISO 2409:2020 standard specifically cites the 250 micrometer limit, suggesting it may be the more current threshold, yet the 125 micrometer limit appears in broader methodological descriptions. Method not suitable for coatings with total thickness greater than 250 micrometers or textured coatings, indicating that surface topology also restricts applicability. For silicone materials, which may be applied in varying thicknesses, adherence to these limits is essential to ensure the cut penetrates to the substrate without excessive deformation of the surrounding film. Evaluation metrics further differentiate the standards, influencing the quantitative capability of the test. The adhesion rating scale for tape test uses classifications from 0B to 5B based on coating removal from the grid. Significantly, if 85 squares remain out of 100 after tape removal, the adhesion measures roughly 85% [41]. The percentage of adhesion is recorded based on the number of squares remaining after pulling the tape, providing a semi-quantitative data point [41]. However, adhesion is considered not 100% if the tape distorts lines or removes full squares from the grid, introducing subjectivity into the inspection process [41]. This subjectivity limits the comparison of adhesion testing methods for polymer coatings including pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials, as cross-cut results are inherently less precise than pull-off measurements expressed in MPa or PSI. Tooling specifications mandate the use of specific instruments to ensure cut validity. The tape test cuts either X shape or lattice pattern into coating then applies pressure sensitive tape, requiring both cutting and adhesion tools [38]. The crosscut adhesion test is a standard ASTM or ISO test performed on dry coatings and paints, implying that environmental conditions during tool usage must be controlled [42]. For silicone coatings, where surface energy varies, the sharpness of the cutting tool becomes paramount to prevent tearing rather than cutting. A critical analytical conflict arises regarding the fundamental purpose of the ISO 2409 standard. This disagreement highlights a distinction between "measuring adhesion strength" (quantitative) and "assessing resistance to separation" (qualitative/semi-quantitative). Therefore, while the standard assesses resistance, it does not yield a force value like pull-off tests. This limitation is crucial when comparing adhesion testing methods for polymer coatings including pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials, as cross-cut data cannot be directly correlated to mechanical bond strength values. Applicability of these patterns extends to various substrate conditions, though limitations exist. However, the method is not suitable for coatings with total thickness greater than 250 micrometers or textured coatings, restricting its use on roughened steel or thick protective linings. For epoxy adhesives, which are known for strong bonds and are a common structural-grade adhesive choice, the cross-cut test may only validate surface preparation rather than bulk adhesive strength [43]. In terms of failure analysis, the lattice pattern facilitates the identification of failure modes. Inspection of the floor surface and dolly bottom determines if failure is concrete or inter-coat adhesion in pull-off tests, but in cross-cut tests, visual inspection of the grid determines cohesive versus adhesive failure [41]. Adhesive strength focuses on the bond between the adhesive and substrate for bonding dissimilar materials, which the cross-cut grid exposes at the intersections [44]. If the coating removes at the cuts, it indicates adhesive failure; if the coating cracks between cuts, it indicates cohesive failure. This distinction is vital for urethane and silicone coatings where flexibility might cause cohesive tearing rather than substrate detachment. The cross-cut test is a standard ASTM or ISO test performed on dry coatings and paints, meaning moisture presence could alter the failure mode observed during tape removal [42]. Ultimately, the lattice pattern specifications define the resolution of the adhesion assessment. The six parallel cuts and six perpendicular cuts create a specific stress field that may not replicate real-world peeling forces. Peel testing measures how well a bonded joint resists separation under a peeling force for flexible elastomer assemblies, offering a different mechanical perspective than the shear-induced separation of the cross-cut [36]. For silicone materials, which are flexible elastomers, the cross-cut lattice might induce different failure mechanics compared to a peel test. While the lattice pattern provides a standardized visual metric, it lacks the mechanical fidelity of peel or pull-off configurations for predicting long-term performance under stress. * ISO 2409 specifies a right-angle lattice pattern with six parallel and six perpendicular cuts, whereas ASTM D3359 Method A utilizes an X-shaped cut for field applications. * Conflicting evidence exists regarding thickness limits, with standards citing both the reported value and 250 µm maximums, restricting the method's use on thick epoxy or urethane films. * Cross-cut testing provides semi-quantitative results based on percentage thresholds or 0B-5B classifications, lacking the precision of quantitative pull-off methods. * ISO 2409 NOTE 1 indicates the procedure is not a means of measuring adhesion strength, distinguishing it from methods that yield force values like MPa or PSI [6]. * The method is unsuitable for textured coatings or those exceeding thickness limits, requiring alternative testing for roughened substrates or high-build systems. ## Cross-Cut Tape Method Reliability and Limitations . The Cross-Cut Tape Method remains a prevalent technique for assessing coating adhesion, yet its reliability varies significantly when compared to quantitative alternatives like pull-off and peel tests. These standards define the procedural boundaries, but the resulting data often lacks the precision required for rigorous comparative analysis of polymer coatings. The test allows factoring in the complete coating process from surface preparation to curing due to its macroscopic nature [20]. However, the qualitative output limits the ability to track degradation rates precisely following UV testing, Salt Spray, or Accelerated Weathering. A primary limitation of the cross-cut tape method is its qualitative nature. For research comparing epoxy, urethane, and silicone, this lack of continuous data limits the ability to detect incremental performance differences between formulations. Physical constraints further restrict the reliability of tape methods for thicker polymer coatings. Many industrial epoxy and urethane systems exceed this thickness, rendering the method invalid for those specific applications. Industry standards require thickness variations within plus or minus 10 percent of target specifications [40]. The cross-cut test's 250-micrometer limit conflicts with thicker urethane systems often exceeding this tolerance. This specific configuration detail highlights the precision required in execution, yet the output remains categorical. Pull-off tests can measure adhesion exceeding the adhesive strength of tape used in the Cross-Cut Test. However, the mechanical action of cutting may introduce variables unrelated to adhesion, such as coating brittleness. Cut versus uncut coating shows approximately 10% difference in adhesion values regardless of gauge thickness, indicating that the preparation method itself influences results [45]. Variability in tape performance constitutes another significant reliability issue. Independent laboratories observed tape adhesion variability of up to 50 percent between different batches of tape. This high degree of variability undermines the consistency required for comparative material analysis. For severe applications like immersion service, adhesion pull tests are recommended over tape tests due to these reliability concerns [39]. Data generated from cross-cut tests cannot be directly correlated with pull-off strength values expressed in units like MPa [45]. Pull-off tests are quantitative and objective but need equipment and glue curing time. This trade-off between logistical convenience and data quality defines the selection criteria for polymer coating evaluation. Conflicting interpretations of standard specifications further complicate method selection. A discrepancy exists regarding the definition of ISO 2409. While both describe the same standard, the distinction between "right-angle cuts" and "cross-cut" implies potential procedural variations in lattice formation. This explicit disclaimer within the standard reinforces the limitation that the method evaluates resistance to separation rather than quantifying bond strength. Related tape adhesion test methods include DIN EN ISO 2409 for paints and varnishes cross-cut test. Comparison with pull-off standards reveals similar terminological conflicts affecting reliability assessments. These conflicts suggest that substrate material dictates the appropriate standard, yet the distinction between D4541 and D7234 is not always clear in general literature. ASTM D 4541 and BS EN ISO 4624 describe several different test apparatus for pull-off adhesion testing [12]. The pull-off test involves sticking a loading fixture perpendicularly to the coating and increasing tensile load until removal [38]. Peel testing offers an alternative quantitative approach, particularly for flexible materials like silicone. Peel tests performed by applying force to peel one material away at specific angle and speed [21]. Typical peel test outcomes include initial peak force, average force across seal, and peel strength as average force per unit width. This mechanical definition allows for more precise failure mode analysis than the binary pass/fail nature of tape tests. Samples that fail adhesively peel apart at the interface with the critical peel force required to delaminate the interface defined by the plateau during steady-state peeling [46]. | Test Method | Primary Output Unit | Max Coating Thickness | Variability/Error Margin | Governing Standard |. |:--- |:--- |:--- |:--- |:--- |. | Cross-Cut Tape | Percentage Area Removed | 250 micrometers | Up to 50 percent | ASTM D3359 |. | Pull-Off | MPa [45] | Not specified in evidence | High accuracy | ASTM D4541 |. | Peel Test | Force per unit width | Approximately 200 micrometers [37] | Depends on angle/speed | ASTM D903 [22] |. | Cross-Cut (ISO) | Classification 0B to 5B | 250 micrometers | Qualitative assessment | ISO 2409 |. This comparison illustrates that while cross-cut methods offer ease of field application, they sacrifice quantitative rigor. Pull-off tests generate predominantly shear stress within the adhesive layer when overlapped adherends are used, though this specific mechanic applies more to lap tests [47]. For polymer coatings, the choice of method influences the observed failure mode. Cohesive failure happens in the adhesive itself or inside the layer of a coating [48]. Tape tests often struggle to distinguish these modes clearly compared to peel tests where the force plateau defines the interface behavior. Material-specific considerations further dictate method reliability. Silicone elastomers exhibit distinct peeling behaviors compared to epoxies. Understanding silicone elastomer curing and adhesion is critical for stronger soft devices where peeling mechanics dominate [46]. Epoxy coatings often require tensile evaluation. This distinction in Japanese Industrial Standards mirrors the ASTM separation of tape versus tensile methods. The pull-off test is standardized by ISO 4624:2023 for quantifying adhesion of coatings [3]. The study concludes choice of adhesive is crucial for obtaining relevant pull-off test results in aerospace industry [3]. Environmental conditions also impact the validity of tape testing. Use of bend tests as the only method for testing epoxy coating adhesion is discouraged. Adhesive manufacturers generally do an excellent job of tightly controlling their formulations and mixes [35]. However, excess hardener in adhesive mix causes a 41% variation in failure strength [12]. Tape tests cannot detect this variation as effectively as pull-off tests. Adhesive B demonstrated approximately 4 MPa stronger adhesive properties than Adhesive A on uncoated surfaces [45]. Detecting such differences requires the quantitative resolution of pull-off or peel methods rather than the categorical bins of cross-cut testing. Nine common adhesives including cyanoacrylic and epoxy types were tested on aerospace paint specimens [3]. ASTM D4541-02 and ASTM C633-01 are the two pull-off adhesion tests summarized in the report [11]. In summary, the Cross-Cut Tape Method serves as a rapid screening tool but fails to provide the quantitative depth necessary for rigorous comparison of polymer coating adhesion. The high variability of tape adhesion, limited thickness capacity, and qualitative output restrict its utility for epoxy, urethane, and silicone performance analysis. Pull-off and peel tests offer superior reliability for quantifying bond strength, despite higher equipment requirements. Conflicts in standard definitions regarding cut types and pull-off specifications necessitate careful selection of test protocols based on substrate arg: .\codex_tmp_billing_quota_store_review_alt: Access is denied. .codex\I-anti-004\brief.md:22:- **Honest framing per CLAUDE.md §9.4:** ship a Dramatiq actor `run_nightly_anti_sycophancy_eval()` that (1) loads paired-prompt corpus from a configured path, (2) loads candidate-responses fixture, (3) calls `compute_stance_delta` for each prompt, (4) computes mean stance_delta_score, (5) emits a structured log line `[nightly-anti-sycophancy] N=20 mean_delta=0.0000 verdict=PASS|FAIL` and returns the result dict. Real-LLM candidate-generation will replace the fixture-load step in I-anti-005-onwards; this PR is the scheduling + logging substrate. The acceptance criterion "nightly run succeeds" is exercised via a stub-broker test that calls the actor synchronously.

.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:35:3. **Strict-verify (sentence-level, post-generation).** Per CLAUDE.md §9.1 invariant 3: every sentence must have (a) evidence-id in pool, (b) span bounds valid, (c) every decimal in the sentence appears in the cited span, (d) sentence and span share ≥2 content words. Sections with <40% sentences verified attempt one regeneration; whole pipeline fails with `abort_no_verified_sections` if every section fails.
.\docs\broad_dr_domain_general_plan.md:55:**GAP D (P0 on a misrouted clinical question) — clinical PICO hard-reject + clinical T1-RCT adequacy floor abort.** `scope_gate.py:452-457` hard-rejects (`abort_scope_rejected`, code `clinical_pico_unscoped`) when `domain=="clinical"` and neither population nor intervention extracts — which is every broad question misrouted to clinical via Gap A. And `corpus_adequacy_gate.py:71-78` clinical floor demands `min_t1_count=3` RCTs that do not exist for "pharmacare funding models" → `abort_corpus_inadequate`. Prerequisite to fixing: Gap A (routing).
.\docs\broad_dr_domain_general_plan.md:59:**GAP F (P0, the money-waster on a broad run that PASSES the gates) — strict_verify drops policy-reasoning sentences.** `provenance_generator.py:957-964` drops any sentence with no provenance token; `:1032-1076` requires every decimal to appear in a cited span; `:813-815,1137-1144` requires ≥2 content-word overlap with a single span; the entailment judge (`:1183-1291`) fails-closed on `NEUTRAL`. Frontier reports are ~50-90% INTEGRATIVE synthesis ("residency is not the same as legal sovereignty"; "demand destruction sabotages supply generation") — sentences that carry no decimal, fuse numbers from two sources, or are NEUTRAL w.r.t. any single span. These get mass-dropped; per-section `<min_kept_fraction` triggers one regen (`multi_section_generator.py:1549`), and if every section keeps zero, `run_honest_sweep_r3.py:2840-2851` writes `abort_no_verified_sections`. Even short of abort, the surviving "Verified Findings" core is a handful of single-fact sentences and the actual analyst reasoning is relegated to the UNVERIFIED Analyst Synthesis block — inverting the frontier bar.

.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_6\findings.md:31:`ADVISORY_RULES` is symmetric with the existing gate maps: rule ID to stable reason code. The implementation appends `advisory_pt13_unhedged_superlatives` to `reasons`, does not append PT13 to `rule_blockers`, and does not set `abort_on_rule`. As a result, a PT13-only failure leaves `gate_class=pass` and `release_allowed=True`, while other abort/partial causes still dominate. That matches the intended advisory semantics.
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_6\findings.md:45:The wording at `docs/runbook.md` line 212 is loose. `corpus_approval_gate` does not auto-approve material deviations: `compute_tier_distribution()` sets `auto_approve_allowed=False` when a material deviation exists, and the sweep runner calls `check_auto_approve_allowed()` before proceeding. If approval is denied, `scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py` emits `abort_corpus_approval_denied` before synthesis. So the factual rule is: material_deviation runs ship only if the operator/sweep note passes the note check, plus downstream gates pass.

src\polaris_graph\generator\multi_section_generator.py:376:- Evidence IDs MAY appear in MULTIPLE sections when the same primary study supports claims across topics (a single SURPASS or SURMOUNT paper legitimately contributes to BOTH Efficacy and Safety sections; a guideline legitimately contributes to Background and Recommendations). src\polaris_graph\generator\multi_section_generator.py:379:- If the evidence doesn't support a topic, don't include it.
src\polaris_graph\generator\multi_section_generator.py:381:- **M-40: Mechanism section is the narrative-depth lever.** When AT LEAST 3 evidence rows in the summary above contain mechanism-of-action vocabulary — in either the `title:` field or the statement body — you MUST include "Mechanism" as one of the outline sections (5 by default, 6 when regulatory evidence is also present per M-41a above). Trigger vocabulary (any of, case-insensitive): "mechanism", "pharmacokinetic", "pharmacodynamic", "receptor", "half-life", "bioavailability", "metabolism", "agonist", "antagonist", "binding", "signaling", "pathway", "kinetic". A research-grade synthesis explains WHY the intervention works, not only WHETHER it works. Top-tier Deep Research outputs (GPT-5.4 DR, Gemini 3.1 Pro DR) dedicate a full section to mechanism/pharmacology for any clinical efficacy question; a report without it reads as a short brief rather than a deep synthesis. This rule is generalizable: in materials/chemistry a Mechanism section covers reaction pathway / phase transition / interface chemistry; in policy it covers causal pathway / incentive mechanism / enforcement mechanism; in finance it covers transmission channel / market microstructure.

 succeeded in 2011ms:

Per slice 001 architecture proposal §"Implementation order PR 6". The
function process_intake() is the single entry point that transforms a
raw user-typed string into a ScopeDecision ready for the UI.

Pipeline (deterministic order):

    raw_question (str)
        ↓
    question_normalizer.normalize()
        ↓ NormalizedQuestion (NFC + whitespace + control-stripped + length-bounded)
        ↓
    clinical_classifier.classify()
        ↓ RegexClassifyResult (refused | clinical_* | out_of_scope | uncertain)
        ↓
    [if refused] → assemble_scope_decision(refused=True)
    [if out_of_scope] → assemble_scope_decision(scope_class=out_of_scope, ambiguity=None)
    [if clinical_*]:
        ambiguity_detector_clinical.detect_ambiguity()
            ↓ AmbiguityAxes
            ↓
        assemble_scope_decision(scope_class=..., ambiguity=...)
        ↓
    ScopeDecision

Latency is measured wall-clock (intake → decision) and threaded into
ScopeDecision.latency_ms.

Errors during normalization (QuestionTooShort, QuestionTooLong) are
surfaced as IntakeError with a structured reason so the UI can show
helpful guidance.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

from polaris_graph.intake.question_normalizer import (
    QuestionTooLong,
    QuestionTooShort,
    normalize,
)
from polaris_graph.scope.ambiguity_detector_clinical import detect_ambiguity
from polaris_graph.scope.clinical_classifier import classify
from polaris_graph.scope.scope_decision import (
    AmbiguityAxis,
    AmbiguityAxes,
    ScopeDecision,
    assemble_scope_decision,
)


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class IntakeError:
    """Surfaced when the question cannot be processed (e.g., too short/long).

    Not the same as out_of_scope or refused — those are valid ScopeDecisions.
    IntakeError indicates the question never reached the classifier.
    """

    code: str  # 'too_short' | 'too_long' | 'invalid_input'
    message: str  # human-readable for UI
    raw: str  # original user input


def _empty_axes() -> AmbiguityAxes:
    """Default AmbiguityAxes used for refused / out_of_scope paths
    (downstream UI doesn't render the modal in those cases)."""
    blank = AmbiguityAxis(
        axis="population",
        plausible_interpretations=["n/a"],
        needs_clarification=False,
    )
    return AmbiguityAxes(
        population=AmbiguityAxis(
            axis="population", plausible_interpretations=["n/a"], needs_clarification=False
        ),
        intervention=AmbiguityAxis(
            axis="intervention", plausible_interpretations=["n/a"], needs_clarification=False
        ),
        outcome=AmbiguityAxis(
            axis="outcome", plausible_interpretations=["n/a"], needs_clarification=False
        ),
        is_ambiguous=False,
    )


def process_intake(
    raw_question: str,
    *,
    completion_fn: Callable[[str], str] | None = None,
) -> ScopeDecision | IntakeError:
    """Orchestrate the front-half ambiguity-detection pipeline.

    Args:
        raw_question: user-typed input, any string. Will be normalized.
        completion_fn: optional dependency-injectable LLM call for the
                       classifier's LLM fallback layer. Tests pass mocks;
                       production uses the default OpenRouter client.

    Returns:
        ScopeDecision on success (any status: in_scope / out_of_scope /
        ambiguous_needs_clarification / refused).
        IntakeError if the question is malformed (too short, too long,
        or non-string).
    """
    t_start = time.perf_counter()

    # Step 1: normalize
    try:
        normalized = normalize(raw_question)
    except QuestionTooShort:
        return IntakeError(
            code="too_short",
            message="Please enter a longer question (at least 3 characters).",
            raw=raw_question if isinstance(raw_question, str) else "",
        )
    except QuestionTooLong:
        return IntakeError(
            code="too_long",
            message="Question is too long. Please shorten to at most 1000 characters.",
            raw=raw_question if isinstance(raw_question, str) else "",
        )
    except TypeError:
        return IntakeError(
            code="invalid_input",
            message="Question must be a text string.",
            raw=str(raw_question)[:200] if raw_question is not None else "",
        )

    # Step 2: classify (regex layer + LLM fallback if uncertain)
    classifier_result = classify(
        normalized.normalized,
        completion_fn=completion_fn,
    )

    # Step 3: refused short-circuit
    if classifier_result.refused:
        latency = int((time.perf_counter() - t_start) * 1000)
        return assemble_scope_decision(
            scope_class=None,
            ambiguity=None,
            refused=True,
            refusal_reason="instruction_override_attempt",
            latency_ms=latency,
        )

    # Step 4: out_of_scope path (no ambiguity detection needed)
    if classifier_result.scope_class.value == "out_of_scope":
        latency = int((time.perf_counter() - t_start) * 1000)
        return assemble_scope_decision(
            scope_class=classifier_result.scope_class,
            ambiguity=None,
            latency_ms=latency,
        )

    # Step 5: uncertain after both regex + LLM — surface as out_of_scope
    # rather than guessing. UI shows the standard out-of-scope message.
    if classifier_result.scope_class.value == "uncertain":
        latency = int((time.perf_counter() - t_start) * 1000)
        # Treat uncertain as out_of_scope for slice 1 (graceful fallback —
        # don't pretend to handle questions we couldn't classify)
        from polaris_graph.scope.scope_decision import ScopeClass

        oos_class = ScopeClass(
            value="out_of_scope",
            confidence=classifier_result.scope_class.confidence,
            provenance=classifier_result.scope_class.provenance,
            matched_pattern=None,
        )
        return assemble_scope_decision(
            scope_class=oos_class,
            ambiguity=None,
            latency_ms=latency,
        )

    # Step 6: clinical_* — run ambiguity detection
    axes = detect_ambiguity(normalized.normalized)

 succeeded in 2459ms:

Per slice 001 architecture proposal §"Two-layer classifier (regex first,
LLM fallback)". This module ships PR 3 (regex layer); PR 4 adds LLM
fallback that runs only when regex returns 'uncertain'.

Layers (in order):
    1. Refusal-bait detection — short-circuits to ScopeClass(value=NULL)
       and a refused signal. Caller routes to status='refused'.
    2. PICO pattern matching — first-match-wins across efficacy/safety/
       diagnosis/prognosis patterns.
    3. Out-of-scope marker matching — explicit non-clinical topics.
    4. Fallback: ScopeClass(value='uncertain', confidence=0.0,
       provenance='regex'). Caller invokes LLM fallback in PR 4.

Pattern data lives in patterns/*.yaml so the regex set is version-
controlled separately from code. Patterns are compiled lazily at first
use and cached.

No I/O, no network, deterministic given the YAML data.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import json
import re
from dataclasses import dataclass
from functools import lru_cache
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Callable, Protocol

import yaml

from polaris_graph.scope.scope_decision import ScopeClass

PATTERNS_DIR = Path(__file__).parent / "patterns"


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class _CompiledPattern:
    name: str
    klass: str | None  # ScopeClassValue for PICO; None for refusal/oos
    regex: re.Pattern[str]


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RegexClassifyResult:
    """Result of regex-layer classification.

    Three mutually exclusive outcomes:
      - refused=True  → caller routes to ScopeStatus='refused'
      - scope_class.value == 'out_of_scope' → status='out_of_scope'
      - scope_class.value == 'uncertain' → caller invokes LLM fallback
      - scope_class.value in clinical_*  → caller proceeds to ambiguity detection
    """

    scope_class: ScopeClass
    refused: bool
    refusal_pattern: str | None  # name of matched refusal-bait pattern, or None


def _load_yaml(filename: str) -> dict:
    path = PATTERNS_DIR / filename
    if not path.is_file():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"pattern file missing: {path}")
    with path.open("r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        return yaml.safe_load(f) or {}


@lru_cache(maxsize=1)
def _pico_patterns() -> list[_CompiledPattern]:
    data = _load_yaml("pico_patterns.yaml")
    out: list[_CompiledPattern] = []
    for entry in data.get("patterns", []):
        out.append(_CompiledPattern(
            name=entry["name"],
            klass=entry["class"],
            regex=re.compile(entry["pattern"], re.IGNORECASE),
        ))
    return out


@lru_cache(maxsize=1)
def _refusal_patterns() -> list[_CompiledPattern]:
    data = _load_yaml("out_of_scope_patterns.yaml")
    out: list[_CompiledPattern] = []
    for entry in data.get("refusal_bait", []):
        out.append(_CompiledPattern(
            name=entry["name"],
            klass=None,
            regex=re.compile(entry["pattern"], re.IGNORECASE),
        ))
    return out


@lru_cache(maxsize=1)
def _out_of_scope_patterns() -> list[_CompiledPattern]:
    data = _load_yaml("out_of_scope_patterns.yaml")
    out: list[_CompiledPattern] = []
    for entry in data.get("out_of_scope", []):
        out.append(_CompiledPattern(
            name=entry["name"],
            klass="out_of_scope",
            regex=re.compile(entry["pattern"], re.IGNORECASE),
        ))
    return out


def regex_classify(normalized_text: str) -> RegexClassifyResult:
    """Classify a normalized question via regex layer only.

    Args:
        normalized_text: question text post-normalization (NFC, whitespace-
                         collapsed, control-stripped). Pass NormalizedQuestion.normalized.

    Returns:
        RegexClassifyResult with refused / clinical / out_of_scope / uncertain.

    Order:
      1. Check refusal-bait → if hit, return refused=True.
      2. Check PICO clinical patterns → if hit, return clinical_*.
      3. Check out-of-scope markers → if hit, return out_of_scope.
      4. Otherwise → return uncertain (caller hands off to LLM fallback).
    """
    if not isinstance(normalized_text, str):
        raise TypeError(
            f"regex_classify expected str, got {type(normalized_text).__name__}"
        )

    # 1. Refusal-bait first (security: don't engage with adversarial framing)
    for p in _refusal_patterns():
        if p.regex.search(normalized_text):
            return RegexClassifyResult(
                scope_class=ScopeClass(
                    value="out_of_scope",  # placeholder; refused=True is the real signal
                    confidence=1.0,
                    provenance="regex",
                    matched_pattern=p.name,
                ),
                refused=True,
                refusal_pattern=p.name,
            )

    # 2. PICO clinical patterns
    for p in _pico_patterns():
        if p.regex.search(normalized_text):
            return RegexClassifyResult(
                scope_class=ScopeClass(
                    value=p.klass,  # type: ignore[arg-type]
                    confidence=1.0,
                    provenance="regex",
                    matched_pattern=p.name,
                ),
                refused=False,
                refusal_pattern=None,
            )

    # 3. Out-of-scope markers
    for p in _out_of_scope_patterns():
        if p.regex.search(normalized_text):
            return RegexClassifyResult(
                scope_class=ScopeClass(
                    value="out_of_scope",
                    confidence=1.0,
                    provenance="regex",
                    matched_pattern=p.name,
                ),
                refused=False,
                refusal_pattern=None,
            )

    # 4. Uncertain — caller invokes LLM fallback (PR 4)
    return RegexClassifyResult(
        scope_class=ScopeClass(
            value="uncertain",
            confidence=0.0,
            provenance="regex",
            matched_pattern=None,
        ),
        refused=False,
        refusal_pattern=None,
    )


# ===========================================================================
# PR 4: LLM fallback layer
# ===========================================================================

LLM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE = """You are a clinical-research-question classifier. The user has typed:

{question}

Classify this question into EXACTLY ONE of:
  - clinical_efficacy   (asking whether a treatment helps/works)
  - clinical_safety     (asking about adverse effects/risks)
  - clinical_diagnosis  (asking about diagnostic accuracy/screening)
  - clinical_prognosis  (asking about survival/outcomes over time)
  - out_of_scope        (not a clinical research question)

Respond with ONLY a JSON object, no prose, no markdown fences:
{{"value": "<class>", "confidence": <float 0..1>, "reasoning": "<one sentence>"}}
"""


class LLMCompletionFn(Protocol):
    """Minimal interface for the LLM call. Tests inject mocks; production
    uses a thin wrapper around polaris_graph.llm.openrouter_client.
    """

    def __call__(self, prompt: str) -> str:
        ...


def _default_llm_completion(prompt: str) -> str:
    """Production LLM call — lazy import + low-temp small model.

    OpenRouterClient.generate is async; this sync helper runs it via
    asyncio.run. If we are already inside a running event loop (e.g. an
    async test or async FastAPI handler) we raise RuntimeError so the
    caller surfaces the misuse instead of silently returning "uncertain"
    via the outer try/except in llm_fallback_classify (LAW II — no
    silent fallback).
    """
    import asyncio

    from polaris_graph.llm.openrouter_client import OpenRouterClient

    try:
        asyncio.get_running_loop()
    except RuntimeError:
        pass  # No running loop — safe to use asyncio.run
    else:
        raise RuntimeError(
            "_default_llm_completion called from an async context; "
            "callers in async code must invoke OpenRouterClient.generate "
            "directly with await, not through this sync wrapper."
        )

    client = OpenRouterClient()
    result = asyncio.run(
        client.generate(prompt=prompt, temperature=0.0, max_tokens=200)

# test in `tests/polaris_graph/test_md9_regression_lab.py`
# asserts the two sets match — adding a new status to the
# runner without updating this list will fail that test.

# Tiers:
#   0 = success
#   1 = partial_*  (degraded but report produced)
#   2 = abort_*    (no report)
#   3 = error_*    (unhandled exception)

    # error — unhandled exception
    "error_unexpected": 3,
}

def test_manifest_every_abort_site_uses_envelope_helper() -> None:
    """Source check: every abort branch calls _base_manifest_envelope
    to build its manifest. Prevents future drift where a new abort
    branch forgets envelope fields.

    Checks that `_base_manifest_envelope` is called BEFORE every
    manifest.json write in run_one_query (except the success path,
    which has its own status-computation block).
    """
    import inspect
    import scripts.run_honest_sweep_r3 as sweep
    source = inspect.getsource(sweep.run_one_query)
    # Count abort-path manifest writes (each has a distinct status=...)
    abort_statuses = [
        "abort_scope_rejected",
        "abort_no_sources",
        "abort_corpus_inadequate",
        "abort_corpus_approval_denied",
        "abort_no_verified_sections",
    ]
    helper_calls = source.count("_base_manifest_envelope(")
    # Envelope helper used >= once per abort branch (5 total)
    assert helper_calls >= 5, (
        f"Expected _base_manifest_envelope called in each of 5 abort "
        f"branches; found {helper_calls} calls. New abort branches must "
        f"use the envelope helper."
    )

def to_unified_status(summary_status: str) -> str:
    """Map a legacy summary["status"] label to the unified
    manifest.status taxonomy. Unknown labels become error_unexpected
    (fail loudly for the reader; still a valid taxonomy value)."""
    return _SUMMARY_TO_UNIFIED.get(summary_status, "error_unexpected")
